request.question: What nationality is the only athlete who has won both Senior All-Ireland Championship medal and this contest in 2005 in Melbourne attended by 91,898 spectators.

title: Tadhg Kennelly
Kerry GAA honours
Emer Dillon
David Curtin
Rachel Moloney
Mark Davoren
Ireland National League (rugby league)
2005 AFL Grand Final
Jenny Duffy
Brian Farrell (Gaelic footballer)
sentences: Tadhg Kennelly (born 1 July 1981) is an Irish sportsperson known for his top level careers in both Gaelic football and Australian rules football.
He is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in both sports.
He has also represented Ireland in the International Rules Series.
Kerry are by far the most successful team in the history of Gaelic football.
They have won 37 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship and have appeared in countless other All-Ireland Football Finals.
Kerry footballers have been garlanded with countless awards and hold numerous individual records in the sport.

Pat Spillane received nine All Stars during a glittering career, a feat matched by no other Gaelic footballer, while Tadhg Kennelly is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in the sports of Australian rules football and Gaelic football.
Here are Kerry's honours.
Emer Dillon born 1984 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, and 2009 and a camogie All Star in 2005.
Emer was selected as player of the match in the 2005 All-Ireland Senior final.
She is also a prominent hockey player and has represented both Munster and Ireland.
She holds a Senior county championship medal with Carrigdhoun and also Junior and under-age championship honours with Ballygarvan.
She has won All-Ireland Senior, Intermediate and Minor medals as well as inter-provincial honours but did not participate in the 2008 championship due to study.
David Curtin (born 13 September 1980) is an inter-county hurling player who plays for Dublin.
He plays his club hurling for Dublin side Ballyboden St. Enda's.
In 2005, he will be remembered for his astounding performance against Laois which confirmed Dublins All-Ireland status for the 2006 championship.
Curtin finished the game with an impressive scoreline of 1-7.
In the 2006 All-Ireland championship, this feat was repeated when Dublin swept aside Westmeath to once retain their Liam McCarthy Cup status.
He finished the 2006 championship with a scoreline of 0-33, a scoreline which left him 5th of the top scorer list despite Dublins lack of progress in the All-Ireland championship.
Curtin was the Dublin hurling captain for 2005.
David was named on the 2006 Dublin Bus/Evening Herald Blue Star hurling XV at Centre Forward.
Curtin won his first Dublin Senior Hurling Championship medal with his club Ballyboden St Endas in October 2007.
Since then Boden have won 4 titles in a row.
The 2007 Dublin championship win qualified Ballyboden to play in their first ever Leinster Senior Club Hurling Championship game against Oulart the Ballagh GAA of Wexford.
Boden won the game by 1-17 to 0-15 with Curtin scoring a total of 0-04.
Rachel Moloney born 1982 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2008, and 2009 and camogie All Star awards in 2005, 2008 and 2009.
She holds Ashbourne Cup medals with U.C.C. as well as Minor, Junior, Intermediate and Senior All-Ireland championship honours with Cork.
Has won inter-provincial honours with Munster and is the side's free-taker and top scorer.
Rachel is coach to her club's Under-14 team.
Mark Davoren is an Irish Gaelic footballer for Dublin and Kilmacud Crokes.
He made his debut for Dublin against Kerry in the National Football League (Ireland) in 2009 scoring a total of 1-03 in the game which finished as a draw in Parnell Park.
He won an All-Ireland Senior Club Football Championship medal with Kilmacud Crokes on St Patricks Day 2009 in Croke Park against Crosmaglen Rangers.
He scored a goal in the game.
Davoren won a Leinster Senior Club Football Championship medal with Crokes against Rhode of Offalyin 2008 at Parnell Park, he scored 1-00 in the final.
He won the Dublin Senior Football Championship in 2008 by beating St Oliver Plunketts/Eoghan Ruadh in Parnell Park scoring a goal in the process.
He won an AFL Dublin Division One title with Kilmacud Crokes in 2008, although he did not make an appearance in the league final.
The All-Ireland Rugby League Championship (now the McGettigans All-Ireland League for sponsorship reasons) was first played for in 1997 under the title All-Ireland Challenge Cup.
Since then it has also been known as the All-Ireland League, Elite League and the All-Ireland Championship.
The 2005 AFL Grand Final was an Australian rules football game contested between the Sydney Swans and West Coast Eagles at the Melbourne Cricket Ground on 24 September 2005.
It was the 109th annual Grand Final of the Australian Football League (formerly the Victorian Football League), staged to determine the premiers for the 2005 AFL season.
The match, attended by 91,898 spectators, was won by Sydney by a margin of four points, marking the club's fourth Premiership and their first since 1933.
Jennifer "Jenny" Duffy (born 1983 in Cork) is a camogie player, footballer and camogie development officer, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2009.
A former student at Cork IT, Jenny is the Camogie Regional Development officer in South Leinster.
She is the holder of All-Ireland championship and league medals with CIT as well as Senior and Junior All-Ireland championship medals and National League.
She won a Senior county championship with her club and captained CIT to the Ashbourne Shield title in 2006.
Brian Farrell is a Gaelic footballer from Nobber, County Meath, Ireland.
He plays with the Meath county team and his local club Nobber.

He first came to national attention when he was part of the Meath team that made it to the 2002 All-Ireland Minor Football Championship final but lost out to Derry having already been beaten by Longford in the Leinster Championship final.
The following year, he was part of the Meath side that won the Leinster and All-Ireland Junior Football Championships.
At club level, he has won County and Leinster Junior medals in 2002 and an All-Ireland Junior Club Football Championship medal in 2003.
In 2010, Farrell won a Meath Intermediate Football Championship medal.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: What nationality is the only athlete who has won both Senior All-Ireland Championship medal and this contest in 2005 in Melbourne attended by 91,898 spectators.
hop1_docs: 2005 AFL Grand Final | The 2005 AFL Grand Final was an Australian rules football game contested between the Sydney Swans and West Coast Eagles at the Melbourne Cricket Ground on 24 September 2005.  It was the 109th annual Grand Final of the Australian Football League (formerly the Victorian Football League), staged to determine the premiers for the 2005 AFL season.  The match, attended by 91,898 spectators, was won by Sydney by a margin of four points, marking the club's fourth Premiership and their first since 1933.

Emer Dillon | Emer Dillon born 1984 in Cork is a camogie player and a marketing executive, winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, and 2009 and a camogie All Star in 2005.  Emer was selected as player of the match in the 2005 All-Ireland Senior final.  She is also a prominent hockey player and has represented both Munster and Ireland.  She holds a Senior county championship medal with Carrigdhoun and also Junior and under-age championship honours with Ballygarvan.  She has won All-Ireland Senior, Intermediate and Minor medals as well as inter-provincial honours but did not participate in the 2008 championship due to study.

Cookstown Fr. Rock's GAC | Cookstown Father Rocks Gaelic Athletic Club is a Gaelic Athletic Association club based in Cookstown in County Tyrone, Northern Ireland.  In recent years Owen Mulligan and Raymond Mulgrew have both won All-Ireland Senior Football Championship medals with the Tyrone senior team.  The club also had five players who won All-Ireland Minor Football Championship medals in the same day in 2004.

2005 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship | The 2005 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship—known as the Foras na Gaeilge All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship for sponsorship reasons—was the high point of the 2005 season in the sport of camogie.  The championship was won for the 21st time by Cork who defeated Tipperary by a four-point margin in the final and became part of the legendary “rebel treble” of 2005 when Cork won the senior hurling, camogie and ladies’ football titles.  The attendance was 14,350.

Ollie Murphy | Ollie Murphy is a Meath Footballer.  He plays club football for Carnaross.  He came to national prominence in 1999 when he was one of Meath's best player's as they won the All-Ireland Senior Football Championship that year, scoring the decisive goal in that year's All Ireland final.  This was his second All Ireland senior medal as he was a substitute when Meath won the 1996 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship.  He is also the holder of 3 Leinster Senior Football Championship medals.  Ollie has won 2 GAA All Stars Awards.  Murphys best season was the 2001 championship where he put in a number of superb display's as Meath reached another All Ireland final.  In that final he had to leave the field of play with a hand injury.  In recent years his form has been not up to what it once was and he has failed to warrant a starting position.  Ollie made his senior inter-county debut playing for London in the 1994 Connacht Championship against Galway.  It was his one and only appearance for the Exiles.  Murphy's Benny father was also an inter-county player, he lined out for his native Fermanagh

Aoife Murray | Aoife Murray (born 1984 in Dunmanway) is a Camogie player and quantity surveyor based in Cork, winner of All Ireland medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, 2008, 2009, 2014 and 2015.  All Star awards in 2004, 2008, 2009, 2012, 2014 and 2015.  Minor, Junior, Intermediate as well as her Senior All-Ireland medals and Munster championship honours in all grades.  Won three county Senior championship medals with Cloughduv and was captain in 2005 for their third county title.  Sister of former Cork Senior hurler, Kevin Murray, who won an All- Ireland medal in 1999.  A series of impressive saves in the 2009 All Ireland final won for her the "player of the match” award from RTÉ.

1989 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship | The 1989 All-Ireland Senior Camogie Championship was won by Killkenny who defeated Cork by an eight-point margin in the final.  The match drew an attendance of 3,024 and marked Angela Downey’s ninth All Ireland medal.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: 2005 AFL Grand Final (attendance 91,898) and named All-Ireland Senior medalists (camogie/football).

- From passage [1]: "2005 AFL Grand Final" — contested between "the Sydney Swans and West Coast Eagles" on "24 September 2005", "attended by 91,898 spectators".
- From passage [2]: "Emer Dillon" — "born 1984 in Cork"; "winner of All Ireland camogie medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, and 2009"; "represented both Munster and Ireland".
- From passage [6]: "Aoife Murray" — "born 1984 in Dunmanway"; "winner of All Ireland medals in 2002, 2005, 2006, 2008, 2009, 2014 and 2015".
- From passage [3]: "Owen Mulligan and Raymond Mulgrew have both won All-Ireland Senior Football Championship medals" (Cookstown Fr. Rock's GAC).
- From passage [5]: "Ollie Murphy" — "won the All-Ireland Senior Football Championship" (1996 substitute, 1999 starter).

Missing / ambiguous facts blocking the answer:
- Missing: the identity of the "only athlete" who has won both a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal and the 2005 AFL Grand Final — no passage links any named All-Ireland medalist to the 2005 Grand Final.
- Ambiguity: "Senior All-Ireland Championship medal" could refer to different codes (camogie, Gaelic football, hurling); the bridge needed is the specific athlete who appears on both the 2005 AFL Grand Final winners' list and as an All-Ireland Senior medal recipient.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. "Tadhg Kennelly" — fetch lede/infobox and Career/Honours sections (candidate who played for Sydney Swans and won an All-Ireland).
2. "2005 AFL Grand Final" — fetch team lists/premiership players or "Sydney Swans 2005 premiership team" section to confirm which players won the match.
3. "2009 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship (Kerry squad)" or "Tadhg Kennelly honours" — lede/infobox or match report to confirm All-Ireland medal.
4. Search keywords if needed: "player who won both AFL premiership and All-Ireland Senior Football Championship" or "Irish player who won 2005 AFL Grand Final and an All-Ireland".
hop2_docs: Tadhg Kennelly | Tadhg Kennelly (born 1 July 1981) is an Irish sportsperson known for his top level careers in both Gaelic football and Australian rules football.  He is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in both sports.  He has also represented Ireland in the International Rules Series.

Noel Kennelly | Noel Kennelly is a footballer from the Listowel Emmets club in North Kerry.  He is a son of former Kerry great Tim Kennelly and older brother of Tadhg Kennelly.  He won an All Ireland Medal with Kerry in 2000.  He won an All Ireland Under 21 medal in 1998 and played in the losing final of 1999, he also played in the losing All Ireland Minor final in 1996.

Listowel Emmets GAA | Listowel Emmets is a Gaelic Athletic Association club based in Listowel, County Kerry, Ireland.  They play in Division 3 of the County Football league and in the Kerry Intermediate Football Championship.  Famous players from the club include Tim Kennelly, and his 2 sons Tadhg and Noel and his famous brother Eamon.
Matt Kennelly | Mathew Luke Kennelly (born 21 March 1989) is an Australian professional baseball catcher.  He is the younger brother of Timothy Kennelly.

Kerry GAA honours | Kerry are by far the most successful team in the history of Gaelic football.  They have won 37 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship and have appeared in countless other All-Ireland Football Finals.  Kerry footballers have been garlanded with countless awards and hold numerous individual records in the sport.  Pat Spillane received nine All Stars during a glittering career, a feat matched by no other Gaelic footballer, while Tadhg Kennelly is the only holder of both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal, the highest possible achievement in the sports of Australian rules football and Gaelic football.  Here are Kerry's honours.

Tadhg Purcell | Tadhg Purcell (born 2 September 1985) is an Irish footballer who plays for Northbridge FC in the National Premier League.  His main position is as a striker, although he can play on the wing.
Tadhg Buidhe mac Tadhg Riabhach Ó Dubhda | Tadhg Buidhe mac Tadhg Riabhach Ó Dubhda, Chief of the Name and Lord of Tireragh, died c. 1457.

response.summary_2: Tadhg Kennelly is Irish. Tadhg Kennelly is the only athlete to hold both an AFL Premiership medallion and a Senior All-Ireland Championship medal.
